issues_found: Label construction using position exclusion is inconsistently applied: sessions where the clicked item appeared in positions 1–2 are excluded from training but retained in test. This means the test set label distribution differs from the training label distribution in a systematic way, which can distort NDCG estimates and make it unclear whether metric differences reflect model quality or the shifted label composition in test.

Circular relevance label dependency with the baseline ranker: since all implicit feedback (clicks) was generated by the production BM25 system, the training labels are conditioned on what BM25 chose to surface. This means the learning-to-rank model is trained on a biased sample of the item space that already reflects BM25's retrieval decisions. Items never retrieved by BM25 have no click signal and are effectively invisible to the new model. This severely limits the experiment's ability to demonstrate that the new model improves upon BM25 for items BM25 systematically under-ranks, not just for re-ranking what BM25 already retrieved.

The seller performance features are described as 'weekly-updated,' but no mechanism is described to ensure that the weekly snapshot used for a given training example corresponds to the seller state at the time of that session. If the most recent seller metrics are naively joined to historical sessions, future seller information leaks backward into training examples from earlier months, contaminating the training set with signals that would not have been available at query time.

The hypothesis explicitly states the model uses 'query-item text embeddings plus recent seller performance signals' and will outperform BM25. The experimental design does not include an ablation that isolates the contribution of text embeddings alone vs. seller signals alone. Without this ablation, even if the new model wins overall, the experiment cannot confirm that semantic text embeddings are doing the work rather than the seller signals, leaving the core hypothesis only partially tested.

The baseline is described as BM25 with seller re-ranking post-processing applied after retrieval, and it is allowed to tune one hyperparameter on the validation set. However, the new model jointly learns to combine text semantics and seller signals end-to-end during training, while the baseline applies them as a two-stage pipeline with limited tuning. This is not a fully fair apples-to-apples comparison: a stronger baseline would be a BM25 retrieval stage followed by a learned re-ranker that uses only seller features, or at minimum a BM25 model with full seller feature integration. The design risks overstating the advantage of the new model by comparing a richly optimized joint model against a loosely tuned pipeline.

CTR at positions 1–3 is listed as a secondary metric, but CTR measured from historical logs is not a clean offline metric — it reflects exposure decisions made by the original BM25 ranker, not the new ranker. Reporting CTR from logs as if it evaluates the new model's ranking is methodologically invalid offline; CTR can only be meaningfully compared in an online setting or through a carefully constructed counterfactual estimator.

The stratification by 'query frequency quintile' and 'seller quality quintile' is applied to ensure representation, but no details are provided on how these quintiles are computed. If query frequency quintiles are computed over the full 18-month dataset before splitting, future query frequency information leaks into the definition of training set strata. Quintiles should be defined using training-period data only and then applied to validation and test.

Months 16–18 as the test period may overlap with or be adjacent to seasonal peaks or promotions that differ structurally from months 1–12 used for training. The design acknowledges non-stationarity but does not describe any check for distributional shift between training and test periods, nor any mechanism to detect whether observed metric differences are driven by temporal distribution shift rather than model quality. Without a distribution shift diagnostic, strong test period gains could be partially artifactual.

The inverse propensity weighting adjustment is described as post-hoc and uses propensity scores presumably estimated from position frequencies in the logs. No detail is given on how propensity scores are estimated, whether they are estimated on the full dataset or only training data, and whether items that were never shown at certain positions have undefined propensities. Poorly estimated propensities can produce high-variance or biased IPS-adjusted metrics that are harder to interpret than the unadjusted ones, yet the design treats IPS adjustment as a reliable confound control.

The design states the embedding model is 'fit once on the training set and applied frozen to validation and test,' but if the embedding model is fine-tuned on the training set query-item pairs (as implied by 'fine-tuned dual-encoder'), then the fine-tuning target — click and purchase labels — is derived from the same implicit feedback used to train the ranker. This creates a risk that the embedding model overfits to the behavioral patterns in training data, and the quality of this fine-tuning step is never separately validated. There is no held-out embedding evaluation or description of how fine-tuning is regularized to prevent overfitting.
smoke_response.reasoning: The design has several strong elements — temporal splitting, preprocessing fit-point discipline, metric diversity, and seller quality controls — but it contains serious issues that undermine its ability to test the stated hypothesis cleanly: the circular dependency of labels on the BM25 retrieval system means the model is evaluated primarily on BM25-surfaced items, the absence of a text-embedding-only ablation leaves the hypothesis only partially testable, potential temporal leakage in seller feature joins could contaminate training labels, and the offline CTR metric is invalid as stated. These issues together are significant enough to require revision before the experiment is executed.
